Issue № 001 · Power & history First essay

The facade of propriety.

An introspection of corruption across systems — from the Radcliffe Line to Operation Cyclone to the algorithmic age. The facade does not contradict the system. The facade enables the system. The facade is the system.

By Taz Punjabi· May 13, 2026· 15 min read
In this essay
i · The Line Drawn in Six Weeks ii · Operation Cyclone iii · The Algorithm as Continuation iv · The Facade Is the System
↗ Full essay on Substack ← All essays

There is a story told about how power operates. It is a story about rules, about legitimacy, about the orderly transfer of authority and the principled application of law. It is a story that has produced functioning institutions, genuine accountability, and real protection for millions of people. It is also, at its edges, precisely false.

Section iThe Line Drawn in Six Weeks

In the summer of 1947, a British lawyer named Cyril Radcliffe was dispatched to South Asia to draw a line. Radcliffe had never been to the subcontinent. He arrived in June and was given six weeks to partition British India into two independent nations — India and Pakistan — by drawing a boundary through the Punjab and Bengal, two of the most densely populated regions on earth.

The line was drawn. It was announced on August 17th, two days after independence, when the border guards had already changed uniforms and the populations had not yet moved. In the months that followed, somewhere between two hundred thousand and two million people were killed in communal violence. Fifteen million people were displaced — the largest forced migration in human history.

The pattern

The facade here is precise: the partition was conducted through proper legal mechanisms, with proper documentation, with proper ceremonies of transfer. Power behaved correctly. The consequences were catastrophic. The correctness of the process did not redeem the outcomes it produced.

Section iiOperation Cyclone

In 1979, the United States government began funding and arming an insurgency in Afghanistan. The program, known as Operation Cyclone, was the largest covert CIA operation in history. Its explicit purpose was to exhaust the Soviet Union by sustaining a prolonged guerrilla war; its architects acknowledged, in retrospect, that the human cost to the Afghan population was understood and accepted as an operational variable.

The mujahideen fighters the program equipped and trained would, in subsequent decades, become the core of Al-Qaeda and the Taliban. The weapons the program supplied would remain in circulation in the region for thirty years. The training infrastructure the program built would outlast the Soviet Union it was designed to pressure.

The facade — in each case — is what allows the system to produce its outcomes while the people who built it retain their dignity and their professional biographies.

Section iiiThe Algorithm as Continuation

The logic that produced the Radcliffe Line and Operation Cyclone did not disappear. It migrated. It now runs in the hiring algorithms that score resumes before a human reads them; in the predictive policing systems that generate risk scores for individuals who have not committed crimes; in the content recommendation engines that optimize for engagement and produce radicalization as a side effect.

The algorithm does not have intentions. That is precisely what makes it so useful.

In every case, the facade is the same: this is a technical system. It is neutral. It operates according to rules. The rules were written down. The process is auditable. The facade functions by converting political questions — who should have access to what, at whose risk — into technical questions, where accountability becomes diffuse and challenge becomes difficult.

Section ivThe Facade Is the System

The key insight — the one that the facade is designed to prevent — is that the facade and the system are not separate. The facade does not cover something that would function differently if uncovered. The facade is the mechanism by which the system recruits its participants, maintains its legitimacy, and insulates its beneficiaries from the cost of what it produces.

Cyril Radcliffe did not impose colonialism on South Asia. He administered its conclusion through proper legal form. The American officials who ran Operation Cyclone did not personally radicalize the Taliban. They funded and trained people who did, through properly authorized covert operations. The technology companies deploying algorithmic systems that produce discriminatory outcomes did not design discrimination. They optimized for business metrics, through a design process that passed legal review.

The facade is what allows the system to produce its outcomes while the people who built and maintained it retain their dignity, their pensions, and their professional biographies. It is not a veneer over something corrupt. It is the active ingredient. It is, ultimately, what needs to be named.

Essay
001
The Facade
The questions
i.Who draws the lines?
ii.Who bears the cost when lines are wrong?
iii.How does a system maintain legitimacy while causing harm?
TP
Taz Punjabi
Thinkers Dilemma is an introspective into the human existence — a newsletter and podcast at the intersection of technology, culture, diaspora experience, and systems thinking. For people who refuse comfortable answers.
